FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


September 20, 2001


Peter D. Moock
Kings County
Office of County Counsel
Government Center
Hanford, CA 93230

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-150

Dear Mr. Moock:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is limited solely to provisions of the Act, and should not be taken as advice or an opinion regarding any other areas of the law potentially raised by your letter. This advice is based upon the facts as provided in your request.² Please note that this letter should not be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A) )

QUESTION

Do Kings County Supervisors Tony Barba, Alene Taylor, Jon Rachford, and Tony Oliveira have disqualifying real property and/or a business interests that prohibit them from participating in the decision to adopt a "Dairy Element" amendment to the county general plan and enact related modifications to the Kings County Zoning Ordinance?³


___________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.
² This advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; § 83114.)
³ For purposes of this letter, the term "Dairy Element" is used to describe the dairy element amendment to the General Plan and the enactment of related modifications to the zoning ordinance.

File No. A-01-150
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. The decision to adopt the Dairy Element will not have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Supervisor Barba's real property interest outside of the
agriculturally zoned areas. As to his indirect real property interest in the agriculturally
zoned area, he can participate in the decision if he determines that it will not have
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property interest. The facts
are insufficient to determine whether his indirect interest in real property in the
agriculturally zoned area will be affected in the same manner as the public generally.

2. The decision to adopt the Dairy Element will not have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Supervisor Taylor's business interests. As to her real property
interest in the agriculturally zoned area, she can participate in the decision if he
determines that it will not have reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her
real property interest. The facts are insufficient to determine whether her interest in real
property in the agriculturally zoned area will be affected in the same manner as the public
generally.

3. Supervisor Rachford may participate in the decision to adopt the Dairy
Element because it will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his
real property and business interests.

4. Supervisor Oliveira may participate in the decision if after examining the
appropriate materiality factors, he determines that adoption of the Dairy Element will
have no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his business interest in a dairy
operation and his real property interests in the agriculturally zoned areas.

FACTS

Milk production has become a major agricultural industry in Kings County.
According to the 1999 Kings County Agricultural Commissioner's Annual Report, dairy
production has been the largest cash crop in Kings County in recent years. Kings County
is ranked as the 12th leading agricultural county in California, the 25th in the nation, and it
is in the fifteen top milk producing counties in the country. Milk represents about 31.8%
of the gross value of agricultural products produced in Kings County.

The existing General Plan and Zoning Ordinance provisions require conditional
use permits for new dairies and for the expansion of existing dairies. The conditional use
permit process requires analysis and review under the California Environmental Quality
Act ("CEQA") for each such individual dairy development project.

The proposed new element of the General Plan is characterized as the "Dairy
Element." It would establish goals, objectives, policies and guidelines that would govern
the development of new milk cow dairies and the expansion of existing milk cow dairies
in Kings County. The preparation and adoption of the Dairy Element would be
accompanied by the preparation and certification of a program environmental impact
report ("PEIR") under CEQA.

File No. A-01-150
Page No. 3

The intent of the Dairy Element is to establish a numerical limit on the total
number of milk cows allowed in the county. Under proposed amendments to the Zoning
Ordinance, dairy development would be allowed up to that limit pursuant to the site plan
review process and pursuant to standards developed in the Dairy Element and in the
PEIR. Each new proposed dairy project would be required to meet the standards set forth
in the Dairy Element and in the PEIR. However, because the site plan review process is a
ministerial act under the Zoning Ordinance, each project would not be subject to further
environmental review under CEQA.

As is the case with the existing General Plan and Zoning Ordinance provisions,
dairy development under the proposed plan and ordinance changes would be allowed to
occur only within the agriculturally zoned areas of the county. There would be no zone
district boundary changes associated with the proposed Dairy Element.⁴ The new
standards for the development of new dairies and the expansion of existing dairies would
not apply to existing dairies which do not expand their operations beyond the limitations
and restrictions set forth in their existing zoning permits.

You have provided a copy of the Notice of Preparation of an Environmental
Impact Report that gives a detailed description of the proposed Dairy Element. It states
that the major purposes of the Dairy Element are as follows:

The first purpose is to ensure that the dairy industry of Kings County continues to
grow and contribute to the economic health of the County. The second purpose is
to ensure that the standards established in the [Dairy] Element protect public
health and safety and the environment.

It is generally believed that adoption of the Dairy Element and the PEIR will
neither increase nor decrease the value of lands in Kings County. It is also believed that
the failure to adopt it will also not result in an increase or a decrease in county land
values. Individuals who develop new dairies or expand existing dairies will find the
administrative review and approval process easier and less costly, and they will not be
required to undertake individual environmental review under CEQA. However, the
standards for development of new dairies and expansion of existing dairies will be the
same under the existing general plan and with the Dairy Element.

The adoption of the Dairy Element only affects areas zoned for agriculture in the
county. Of a total of 60,286 parcels owned in the county, 21,868 parcels are located in
agriculturally zoned areas. Of a total a 38,099 individual landowners in the county, 9,705
individuals own land in the agriculturally zoned areas.

The Kings County Board of Supervisors must approve the Dairy Element as a
modification to the General Plan and enact related modifications to the Kings County
Zoning Ordinance. Your request for advice pertains to potential conflicts of interest for
four members of the board of supervisors in making that decision.

⁴ In a telephone call with you, it was confirmed that there would be no rezoning of any districts if
the Dairy Element is adopted.

File No. A-01-150
Page No. 4

Supervisor Tony Barba

Supervisor Tony Barba and his wife own 2½ acres land in a rural residential
estates ("RRE") zone district. This land is currently planted with walnut trees. Dairies
are not presently in the RRE district, and would still not be allowed if the Dairy Element
is adopted.

Supervisor Barba's wife owns a one-fifth interest in 50 acres of farmland that is
zoned as "General Agricultural." Supervisor Barba's wife receives approximately
$800.00 per year in leasehold income from her interest in this property. This land is
within the territory that would be affected by adoption of the Dairy Element, but the
zoning of the land would not be changed.

Supervisor Alene Taylor

Supervisor Alene Taylor and her husband own 80 acres that is currently zoned as
"General Agricultural." This land is within the territory that would be affected by
adoption of the Dairy Element, but the zoning of the land would not change. Supervisor
Taylor and her husband are currently farming this land in partnership with another
couple. It is planted in corn that will be sold to the dairy industry for approximately
$20,000 to $25,000.

Supervisor Taylor also has an interest in her husband's co-ownership of an
excavation company in the county. This company did $112,284 in business in the county
of which $12,160 is attributable to the dairy industry.

Supervisor Jon Rachford

Supervisor Jon Rachford has an ownership interest in a Subchapter S corporation
which leases land for production of row crops. This land is zoned as "General
Agricultural" and is within the territory that would be affected by adoption of the Dairy
Element, but the zoning of the land would not be changed. The activities of the
Subchapter S corporation are not in any way related to the dairy industry.

Supervisor Rachford is also a licensed civil engineer, engineering/real property
consultant, and real estate broker. A portion of the income from these business interests
comes from agriculture-related work and from brokerage/agent fees for the sale of
agricultural lands. However, to date none of these business interests has been involved in
the dairy industry in the county, nor have they been involved in the development of new
dairies or the expansion of existing dairies.

Supervisor Tony Oliveira

Supervisor Tony Oliveira and his wife each have a 1/8 interest in a Subchapter S
corporation which owns and operates a dairy on land zoned as "Exclusive Agricultural."
The dairy is within the territory that would be affected by adoption of the Dairy Element,

File No. A-01-150
Page No. 5

but the zoning of the land would not be changed. He and his wife, respectively receive
1/8 of the income generated by dairy business.

Supervisor Oliveira also owns and leases other lands in the vicinity of the dairy,
which he farms. These lands are also zoned as "Exclusive Agricultural." They are
planted in row crops.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. (§ 87100.) Pursuant to Regulation 18700, an eight-
step analysis is applied to determine whether a public official has a conflict of interest in
a given governmental decision.

Steps One-Three: Is the individual a "public official"? Is the public official making,
participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision? What is the
"economic interest" of the public official?

Your letter correctly concludes that each of the four members of the board of
supervisors, in deciding whether or not to approve the adoption of the "Dairy Element"
amendment to the general plan and attendant zoning ordinance changes, is a public
official making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision.
(§ 82048, Regulations 18701, 18702, 18702.1.) You are also correct that each of the
members has either an economic interest in real property and/or a business entity that is
affected by the decision. (Regulations 18703.1, 18703.2.)

Step Four: Are the public official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved
in the decision?

As each supervisor has distinct economic interest(s) that may be affected by the
decision to adopt or not to adopt the "Dairy Element" amendment to the General Plan and
related Zoning Ordinance changes, we analyze each member's economic interest(s)
separately to determine if they are directly or indirectly involved in the decision to adopt
the Dairy Element amendment to the General Plan and enact related Zoning Ordinance
changes.

Supervisor Tony Barba

"Real property in which a public official has an economic interest, is directly
involved in a governmental decision if that real property is the subject of the
governmental decision, or if any part of that real property is located within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the real property which is the subject of the
governmental decision." (Regulation 18704.2.)

Supervisor Barba has an economic interest in the 2½ acres of land in the RRE
zoned district. This land is not being rezoned and is not otherwise directly involved in

File No. A-01-150
Page No. 6

the governmental decision to adopt the Dairy Element. (Regulation 18704.2(a)(1)-(6).)
As such, Supervisor Barba's economic interest in this real property is indirectly involved
in the governmental decision.

Supervisor Barba has an indirect financial interest in his wife's one-fifth interest
in the 50 acres of farmland zoned as "General Agricultural." (§ 87103(b); Becks Advice
Letter, No. A-97-209.) While this real property is in the area which is affected by the
adoption of the Dairy Element, it would not be rezoned and is otherwise not directly
involved in the governmental decision to adopt the Dairy Element. (Regulation
18704.2(a)(1)-(6).) Again, Supervisor Barba's economic interest in this real property is
indirectly involved in the governmental decision.

Based on the facts that you have provided, Supervisor Barba's community
property share of income from his wife's 50 acres of farmland does not meet the income
threshold of $500.00, to constitute an economic interest in the property as a source of
income.⁵ (§ 87103(c).)

Supervisor Alene Taylor

Supervisor Taylor has a direct interest in the 80 acres of real property that she
owns with her husband. (§ 87103(b).) While this real property is in the area which is
affected by the adoption of the Dairy Element, it would not be rezoned and is otherwise
not directly involved in the governmental decision to adopt the Dairy Element. (Regulation
18704.2(a)(1)-(6).) Supervisor Taylor has a business interest in the farming of this land
which is indirectly involved in the governmental decision. (Regulation 18704.1.)

Supervisor Taylor has an indirect business interest in her husband's co-ownership
of an excavation business. (§ 87103(a).) This business interest is indirectly involved in
the governmental decision. (Regulation 18704.1.)

Supervisor Jon Rachford

Supervisor Rachford has a business interest in the Subchapter S corporation
which leases land for the production of row crops. (§ 87103(a).) While this land, which is
leased and farmed by the corporation is in the area that is affected by the adoption of the
Dairy Element, the corporation as a business entity would be indirectly involved in the
governmental decision. (Regulation 18704.1.) Similarly, his business interests as a civil
engineer, engineering/real property consultant, and real estate broker would all be
indirectly involved in the governmental decision. (Ibid.)

⁵ The $500.00 threshold under § 87103(c) is an aggregate income amount over the twelve months
prior to the governmental decision. You have provided no information as to other income generated from
this property besides the approximate $800.00 in leasehold income; and it is assumed for purposes of this
analysis that even if aggregated, the community property share of income from the property does not
exceed $400.00 per year.

File No. A-01-150
Page No. 7

Supervisor Tony Oliveira

Supervisor Oliveira has a direct business interest by virtue of his 1/8 ownership
interest of the Subchapter S corporation that owns and operates a dairy on land zoned as
"Exclusive Agricultural." (§ 87103(a).) He also has an indirect business interest by
virtue of his wife's 1/8 ownership interest of the same Subchapter S corporation. (Ibid.)
These interests are indirectly involved in the governmental decision to adopt the Dairy
Element. (Regulation 18704.2.)

He has a direct interest in real property in the lands which he owns and leases in
the vicinity of the dairy.⁶ (87103(b).) This interest is also indirectly involved in the
governmental decision. (Regulation 18704.1.)

Steps Five and Six: Will the financial effect of the decision on the public official's
economic interests be material and reasonably foreseeable?

Whether the financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's
economic interest is material and reasonably foreseeable depends upon the facts and
circumstances of each particular case. An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if
there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if
the effect is a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 198.)

Supervisor Tony Barba

In examining the effect of the decision to adopt the Dairy Element on Supervisor
Barba's real property interests, "[t]he financial effect of a governmental decision on real
property which is indirectly involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be
material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific
circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of
the real property in which the public official has an economic interest, which make it
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the real
property in which the public official has an interest." (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1).)
Supervisor Barba's interest in 2½ acres of land in the RRF district is not in a zone that
will be affected by adoption of the Dairy Element. Nothing in the information provided
by you otherwise indicates this real property interest will in any way be affected by the
adoption of the Dairy Element.

Supervisor Barba's indirect economic interest in his wife's 1/5 ownership interest
in 50 acres of farmland is governed by the materiality standard for real property indirectly
involved in a governmental decision. As stated above, the financial effect of the
governmental decision "is presumed not to be material." (Reg. 18705.2.) As this real
property is located in the area affected by the decision, Supervisor Barba must determine
whether the presumption of nonmateriality is rebutted. Examples of the circumstances to
be considered in this regard are:

⁶ These holdings may also constitute a financial interest as a source of income, but no facts have
been provided in that regard. (§ 87103(c).)

File No. A-01-150
Page No. 8

(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real property
in which the official has an economic interest;
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic interest;
(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to, substantial
effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or
similar traits of the neighborhood. (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1)(A)-(C).)

If these factors are considered, and there is no material financial effect on
Supervisor Barba's interest in the real property, he can participate in the decision. As a
public official, he "must make a good faith effort to assess the effect of the decision by
using some reasonable and objective method of valuation. A public official is not
required to, by may choose to obtain a professional appraisal, such as a real estate
appraisal, to assist in assessing the financial effect of a decision for conflict of interest
purposes." (Ho Advice Letter, No. A-00-241.)

Supervisor Alene Taylor

In a similar vein, the 80 acres which Supervisor Taylor and her husband own are
governed by the materiality standard for real property indirectly involved in a
governmental decision and are presumed not to be material. (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1).)
However, like Supervisor Barba, her real property is in the area affected by the decision,
and she would need to examine the specific circumstances of the governmental decision
as they relate to her property to determine if the presumption is rebutted. (Regulation
18705.2(b)(1)(A)-(C).)

Her business interest in the farm operation on the land is governed by the
materiality standard for business interests that are indirectly involved in a governmental
decision. (Regulation 18705.1(b).)The financial effect of a governmental decision on the
farming operation is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more, or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value
of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more." (Regulation
18705.1(c)(4)(A)-(C).)

While it is true that the land is currently farmed in corn to be sold to the dairy
industry, as discussed below, it is highly speculative how the Dairy Element's facilitation
of construction of new dairies or expansion of existing dairies would cause a financial
effect within the scope of the materiality standard set forth above. (See In re Thorner,
supra, 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) As the operation expects to gross only $20,000-$25,000 to
begin with, it is not reasonably foreseeable that its gross business revenues would be

File No. A-01-150
Page No. 9

increased by $20,000 because of the adoption of the Dairy Element. Further, nothing in
the facts indicates that the other two materiality factors for business would be affected by
the adoption of the Dairy Element.

With regard to her interest in her husband's co-ownership of a commercial
excavation company, this is also governed by the materiality standard for business
interests which are indirectly involved in a governmental decision. (Regulation
18705.1(c).) The excavation company does $12,160 in yearly business with the county
dairy industry. It is reasonably foreseeable that the adoption of the Dairy Element will
facilitate construction of new dairies and expansion of existing dairy operations. The
same materiality standard applicable to her farming operation would be applied to her
business interest in the excavation company. (Regulation 18705.1(c)(4)(A)-(C).) Based
on the excavation business' limited reliance on the dairy industry as a source of business,
it is not reasonably foreseeable that its gross business revenues would be increased by
$20,000 because of the adoption of the Dairy Element. Further, nothing in the facts
indicates that the other two materiality factors for business would be affected by the
adoption of the Dairy Element. (See In re Thorner, supra, 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Supervisor Jon Rachford

Supervisor Rachford's ownership of a Subchapter S corporation which leases land
for the production of row crops is governed by the materiality standard for business
interests which are indirectly involved in a governmental decision. (Regulation
18705.1(c).) The information which you have provided to the effect that the activities of
the corporation are not in any way involved with the dairy industry supports the
conclusion the decision on the adoption of the Dairy Element would have no
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on this business interest. (See Regulation
18705.1(c)(4)(A)-(C).)

With regard to his leasehold interest in real property farmed by the corporation,
this interest is governed by the materiality standard for leasehold interests that are
indirectly involved in a governmental decision and is presumed not to be material.
(Regulation 18705.2(b)(2).) In this regard the materiality standard and factors to be
considered are as follows:

"This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances
regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real
property in which the public official has an economic interest, which make it
reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will:

"(A) Change the legally allowable use of the leased real property, and the lessee
has a right to sublease the real property;

"(B) Change the lessee's actual use of the real property;

"(C) Substantially enhance or significantly decrease the lessee's use or enjoyment
of the leased real property;

File No. A-01-150
Page No. 10

"(D) Increase or decrease the amount of rent for the leased real property by 5+
percent during any 12-month period following the decision; or

"(E) Result in a change in the termination date of the lease."
(Regulation 18705.2(b)(2).)

Nothing in the facts that have been provided indicates that any of these changes will be
reasonably foreseeable because of the adoption of the "Dairy Element." As such, the
decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Supervisor
Rachford's leasehold interest.

Supervisor Rachford's interests as a civil engineer, engineering consultant and
real estate broker would also be governed by the materiality standard for business
interests that are indirectly involved in a governmental decision. (Regulation 18705.1(c).)
While you have indicated that a portion of the income for these businesses comes from
agriculture-related work, you have provided additional information to the effect that to
date none of these business interests have in any way been involved with the dairy
industry. Based on the information provided, the decision on the adoption of the Dairy
Element would have no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on this business
interest. (Regulation 18705.1(c)(4)(A)-(C).)

Supervisor Tony Oliveira

Supervisor Oliveira's interest in a Subchapter S corporation which operates a
dairy on land affected by adoption of the Dairy Element is governed by the materiality
standard for business interests that are indirectly involved in a governmental decision.
(Regulation 18705.1(c).) One the results of adoption the Dairy Element would be that the
expansion of existing dairies would be less costly because there would be no requirement
to undertake an individual environmental review under the California Environmental
Quality Act (CEQA). In light of Supervisor Oliveira's interest in the dairy and his related
leasehold and real property ownership interests it is reasonably foreseeable that the
Subchapter S corporation in which he has an interest will at some point expand or
otherwise alter its operations in a manner in which it benefits financially from the
adoption of the Dairy Element. As such, Supervisor Oliveira must determine whether the
financial effect on his business interest in the dairy operation requires that he disqualify
himself from participating in the decision to adopt the Dairy Element. 7 (See Regulation
18705.1(c)(4)(A)-(C).)

With regard to his leasehold and ownership interests in land in the vicinity of that
dairy operation, these interests would be subject to the materiality standards for real
property that are indirectly involved in a governmental decision and are presumed not to
be material. Supervisor Oliveira would need to examine the specific circumstances of the

7 No information has been provided as to the actual amount of the projected cost savings under the
Dairy Element for existing dairies seeking to expand their operations.

File No. A-01-150
Page No. 11

governmental decision as they relate to his leasehold and ownership interests in the real
property to determine if the presumption is rebutted. (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1)(2).).

If these factors are considered, and there is no reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on Supervisor Oliveira's interests in the real property, he can participate
in the decision. As a public official, he "must make a good faith effort to assess the effect
of the decision by using some reasonable and objective method of valuation. A public
official is not required to, but may choose to obtain a professional appraisal, such as a
real estate appraisal, to assist in assessing the financial effect of a decision for conflict of
interest purposes." (Ho Advice Letter, supra, No. A-00-241.)

Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?

In your letter, you have set forth facts suggesting application of the "public
generally" exception to the four supervisors' decision to adopt the "Dairy Element." In
further telephone conversations, you provided additional information in this regard.
Under the "public generally" exception, an official may still participate in a decision if
the financial effect of the decision on the official's economic interest is indistinguishable
from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a) )
This "public generally" exception is codified in Regulations 18707-18707.9. Pursuant to
these provisions, if a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction is affected by the
governmental decision in substantially the same manner as it would affect the public
official, then the official may participate in the decision.

For purposes of application of the "public generally" to a decision involving a real
property interest, a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction is defined as follows:

"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official represents, or
"(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official's
agency. (Regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(B)(i)(ii) )

The adoption of the Dairy Element only affects areas zoned for agriculture in the
county. Of a total of 60,286 parcels owned in the county, 21,868 parcels are located in
agriculturally zoned areas. Of a total a 38,099 individual landowners in the county, 9,705
individuals own land in the agriculturally zoned areas. The adoption of the Dairy
Element will clearly affect either ten percent of all property owners or 5,000 property
owners in the county. As such, in terms of its effect on real property interests in the
county, the adoption of the Dairy Element affects a "significant segment" of the
jurisdiction.

The information that you have provided is insufficient to determine whether the
effects on Supervisor Barba's and Supervisor Taylor's interests in real property in the
agriculturally zoned area are substantially the same as the effects on the public generally. 8

8 Supervisors Barba, Taylor, and Oliveira may not reach this issue if they determine that there is
no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their real property interests as discussed above.

File No. A-01-150
Page No. 12

(Regulation 18707(b)(4).) The effects may be different depending on the size of the
parcels. However, because Supervisor Oliveira is operating a dairy with attendant
improvements on the property and has other dairy related real property interests, all of
which will be specifically affected by the adoption of the Dairy Element in terms of
future expansion, his real property interests will be affected in a manner that is clearly
different from the effect on the public generally.

The regulations define a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction in relation to
business interests as follows:

For decisions that affect a business entity in which a public official has an
economic interest the decision also affects 2,000 or twenty-five percent of all
business entities in the jurisdiction or the district the official represents, so long as
the effect is on persons composed of more than a single industry, trade, or
profession.... (Regulation 18707.1(b)(1)(C).)

The information that you have provided with regard to the effect of the decision
does not establish that it also affects 2,000 or twenty-five percent of all business entities
in the county. As such, there is no basis to apply the "public generally" exception to the
decision to adopt the Dairy Element as it applies to the business interests of the
supervisors.⁹

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: William L. Williams, Jr.
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

WLW:jg
I:\AdviceLtrs\01-150

⁹ You have suggested that the dairy industry is a "predominant industry" in Kings County;
however, the data that you have provided in this regard does not support this contention. (Regulation
18707.7(b).) You have also suggested that the board of supervisors' jurisdiction for purposes of adoption of
the Dairy Element is only the unincorporated area of the county. The Commission has previously expressly
rejected that contention. (In re Legan (1985) 9 FPPC Ops. 1.)

sections.question:
Do Kings County Supervisors Tony Barba, Alene Taylor, Jon Rachford, and Tony
Oliveira have disqualifying real property andlor a business interests that prohibit them
from participating in the decision to adopt a"Dairy Element" amendment to the county
general plan and enact related modifications to the Kings County Zoning Ordinance? 
3
2 This advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are
coffect, and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71; g
83 r 14.)
' For purposes of this letter, the term "Dairy Element" is used to describe the dairy element
amendment to the General Plan and the enactment of related modifications to the zoning ordinance.
sections.conclusion:
1. The decision to adopt the Dairy Element will not have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Supervisor Barba's real property interest outside of the
agriculturally zoned areas. As to his indirect real property interest in the agriculturally
zoned area, he can participate in the decision if he determines that it will not have
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property interest. The facts
are insufficient to determine whether his indirect interest in real property in the
agriculturally zoned area will be affected in the same manner as the public generally.
2 The decision to adopt the Dairy Element will not have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Supervisor Taylor's business interests. As to her real property
interest in the agriculturally zoned area, she can particip ate in the decision if he
determines that it will not have reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her
real property interest. The facts are insufficient to determine whether her interest in real
property in the agriculturally zoned area will be affected in the same manner as the public
generally.
3. Supervisor Rachford may participate in the decision to adopt the Dairy
Element because it will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his
real property and business interests.
4. Supervisor Oliveira may participate in the decision if after examining the
appropriate materiality factors, he determines that adoption of the Dairy Element will
have no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his business interest in a dairy
operation and his real property interests in the agriculturally zoned areas.
sections.facts:
Milk production has become a major agricultural industry in Kings County.
According to the 1999 Kings County Agricultural Commissioner's Annual Report, dairy
production has been the largest cash crop in Kings County in recent years. Kings County
is ranked as the 12tr leading agricultural county io CAifoinia, the 25ih inthe nation, and it
is in the fifteen top milk producing counties in the country. Milk represents about 31.8%
of the gross value of agricultural products produced in Kings County.
The existing General Plan and Zoning Ordinance provisions require conditional
use permits for new dairies and for the expansion of existing dairies. The conditional use
permit process requires analysis and review under the California Environmental Quality
Act ("CEQA") for each such individual dairy development project.
The proposed new element of the General Plan is characterrzed as the "Dairy
Element." It would establish goals, objectives, policies and guidelines that would govern
the development of new milk cow dairies and the expansion of existing milk cow dairies
in Kings county. The preparation and adoption of the Dairy Element would be
accompanied by the preparation and certification of a program environmental impact
report ("PEIR") under CEQA.
The intent of the Dairy Element is to establish a numerical limit on the total
number of milk cows allowed in the county. Under proposed amendments to the Zoning
Ordinance, dairy development would be allowed up to that limit pursuant to the site plan
review process and pursuant to standards developed in the Dairy Element and in the
PEIR. Each new proposed dairy project would be required to meet the standards set forth
in the Dairy Element and in the PEIR. However, because the site plan review process is a
ministerial act under the Zoning Ordinance, each project would not be subject to further
environmental review under CEQA.
As is the case with the existing General Plan and Zoning Ordinance provisions,
dairy development under the proposed plan and ordinance changes would be allowed to
oocur only within the agriculturally zoned areas of the county. There would be no zone
district boundary changes associated with the proposed Dairy Element.a The new
standards for the development of new dairies and the expansion of existing dairies would
not apply to existing dairies which do not expand their operations beyond the limitations
and restrictions set forth in their existing zoningpermits.
You have provided a copy of the Notice of Preparation of an Environmental
Impact Report that gives a detailed description of the proposed Dairy Element. It states
that the major purposes of the Dairy Element are as follows:
The first purpose is to ensure that the dairy industry of Kings County continues to
grow and contribute to the economic health of the County. The second purpose is
to ensure that the standards established in the [Dairy] Element protect public
health and safety and the environment.
It is generally believed that adoption of the Dairy Element and the PEIR will
neither increase nor decrease the value of lands in Kings County. It is also believed that
the failure to adopt it will also not result in an increase or a decrease in county land
values. Individuals who develop new dairies or expand existing dairies will find the
administrative review and approval process easier and less costly, and they will not be
required to undertake individual environmental review under CEQA. However, the
standards for development of new dairies and expansion of existing dairies will be the
same under the existing general plan and with the Dairy Element.
The adoption of the Dairy Element only affects areas zoned for agriculture in the
county. Of a total of 60,286 parcels owned in the county, 21,868 parcels are located in
agriculturally zoned areas. Of a total a38,099 individual landowners in the county, 9,705
individuals own land in the agriculturally zoned areas.
The Kings County Board of Supervisors must approve the Dairy Element as a
modification to the General Plan and enact related modifications to the Kings County
Zoning Ordinance. Your request for advice pertains to potential conflicts of interest for
four members of the board of supervisors in making that decision.
o L , t"l"phone call with you, it was confirmed that there would be no rezoning of any districts if
the Dairy Element is adopted.
Supervisor Tony Barba
Supervisor Tony Barba and his wife own ZYz acres land in a rural residential
estates ("RRE") zone district. This land is currently planted with walnut trees. Dairies
are not presently in the RRE district, and would still not be allowed if the Dairy Element
is adopted.
Supervisor Barba's wife owns a one-fifth interest in 50 acres of farmland that is
zoned as "General Agricultural." Supervisor Barba's wife receives approximately
$800.00 per year in leasehold income from her interest in this property. This land is
within the territory that would be affected by adoption of the Dairy Element, but the
zontng of the land would not be changed.
Supervisor Alene Ta)zlor
Supervisor Alene Taylor and her husband own 80 acres that is currently zoned as
"General Agricultural." This land is within the territory that would be affected by
adoption of the Dairy Element, but the zoning of the land would not change. Supervisor
Taylor and her husband are currently farming this land in partnership with another
couple. It is planted in corn that will be sold to the dairy industry for approximately
$20,000 to $25,000.
Supervisor Taylor also has an interest in her husband's co-ownership of an
excavation company in the county. This company did $712,284 in business in the county
of which $12,160 is attributable to the dairy industry.
Supervisor Jon Rachford
Supervisor Jon Rachford has an ownership interest in a Subchapter S corporation
which leases land for production of row crops. This land is zoned as "General
Agricultural" and is within the teritory that would be affected by adoption of the Dairy
Element, but the zoning of the land would not be changed. The activities of the
Subchapter S corporation are not in any way related to the dairy industry.
Supervisor Rachford is also a licensed civil engineer, engineeringlreal property
consultant, and real estate broker. A portion of the income from these business interests
comes from agriculture-related work and from brokerage/agent fees for the sale of
agricultural lands. However, to date none of these business interests has been involved in
the dairy industry in the county, nor have they been involved in the development of new
dairies or the expansion of existing dairies.
Supervisor Tony Oliveira
Supervisor Tony Oliveira and his wife each have a 1/8 interest in a Subchapter S
corporation which owns and operates a dairy on land zoned as "Exclusive Agricultural."
The dairy is within the territory that would be affected by adoption of the Dairy Element,
but the zoning of the land would not be changed. He and his wife, respectively receive
1/8 of the income generated by dairy business.
Supervisor Oliveira also owns and leases other lands in the vicinity of the dairy,
which he farms. These lands are also zoned as "Exclusive Agricultural." They are
planted in row crops.
sections.analysis:
The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest. ($ 87100.) Pursuant to Regulation 18700, an eight-
step analysis is applied to determine whether a public official has a conflict of interest in
a given governmental decision.
Steps One-Three: Is the individual a "public official?" Is the public official making,
participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision? What is the
"economic interest" of the public olficial?
Your letter correctly concludes that each of the four members of the board of
supervisors, in deciding whether or not to approve the adoption of the "Dairy Element"
amendment to the general plan and attendant zoning ordinance changes, is a public
official making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision.
($ 820a8; Regulations 18701, 18702,18702.1.) You are also corectthat each of the
members has either an economic interest in real property andlor a business entity that is
affected by the decision. (Regulations 18703.1, 18703.2.)
Step Four: Are the public official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved
in the decision?
As each supervisor has distinct economic interest(s) that may be affected by the
decision to adopt or not to adopt the "Dairy Element" amendment to the General Plan and
related Zoning Ordinance changes, we analyze each member's economic interest(s)
separately to determine if they are directly or indirectly involved in the decision to adopt
the Dairy Element amendment to the General Plan and enact related Zoning Ordinance
changes.
Supervisor Tony Barba
"Real property in which a public official has an economic interest, is directly
involved in a governmental decision if that real property is the subject of the
governmental decision, or if any part of that real property is located within 500 feet of the
boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the real property which is the subject of the
governmental decision. " (Regulatron I87 04.2 )
Supervisor Barba has an economic interest in the 2Yz acres of land in the RRE
zoned district. This land is not being rezoned and is not otherwise directly involved in
the governmental decision to adopt the Dairy Element. (Regulation 1870a.2@)(1)-(6) )
As such, Supervisor Barba's economic interest in this real property is indirectly involved
in the governmental decision.
Supervisor Barba has an indirect financial interest in his wife's one-fifth interest
in the 50 acres of farmland zoned as "General Agricultural." ($ 87103(b); Becks Advice
Letter, No. 4-97-209.) While this real property is in the area which is affected by the
adoption of the Dairy Element, it would not be rezoned and is otherwise not directly
involved the governmental decision to adopt the Dairy Element. (Regulation
1870a.2@)(1)-(6) ) Again, Supervisor Barba's economic interest in this real property is
indirectly involved in the governmental decision.
Based on the facts that you have provided, Supervisor Barba's community
property share of income from his wife's 50 acres of farmland does not meet the income
threshold of $500.00, to constitute an economic interest in the property as a source of
income.5 (g 87103(c).)
Supervisor Alene Taylor
Supervisor Taylor has a direct interest in the 80 acres of real property that she
owns with her husband. ($ 87103(b) ) While this real property is in the area which is
affected by the adoption of the Dairy Element, it would not be rezoned and is otherwise
not directly involved the governmental decision to adopt the Dairy Element. (Regulation
1870a.2@)(1)-(6) ) Supervisor Taylor has a business interest in the farming of this land
which is indirectly involved in the governmental decision. (Regulation 18704. 1.)
Supervisor Taylor has an indirect business interest in her husband's co-ownership
of an excavation business. ($ 87103(a).) This business interest is indirectly involved in
the governmental decision. (Regulation 18704. 1.)
Supervisor Jon Rachford
Supervisor Rachford has a business interest in the Subchapter S corporation
which leases land for the production of row crops. ($ 87103(a).) While this land, which is
leased and farmed by the corporation is in the areathat is affected by the adoption of the
Dairy Element, the corporation as a business entity would be indirectly involved in the
governmental decision. (Regulationl8704.1.) Similarly, his business interests as a civil
engineer, engineering/real property consultant, and real estate broker would all be
indirectly involved in the governmental decision. (Ibid.)
5 Tlie $S00.00 threshold under $ 87103(c) is an ag$egateincome anount over the twelve months
prior to the governmental decision. You have provided no information as to other income generated from
this property besides the approximate $800.00 in leasehold income; and it is assumed for purposes of this
analysis that even lf aggregaled. the communlty property share of income from the property does not
exceed $400.00 per year.


File No A-01-150
Supervisor Tonv Oliveira
Supervisor Oliveira has a direct business interest by virtue of his 1/8 ownership
interest of the Subchapter S corporation that owns and operates a dairy on land zoned as
"Exclusive Agricultural." ($ 87103(a).) He also has an indirect business interest by
virtue of his wife's 1/8 ownership interest of the same Subchapter S corporation. (Ibid.)
These interests are indirectly involved in the governmental decision to adopt the Dairy
Element. (Regulation l8l 04.2.)
He has a direct interest in real property in the lands which he owns and leases in
the vicinity of the dairy." (87103(b).) This interest is also indirectly involved in the
governmental decision. (Regulation 18704. l.)
Steps Five and Six: Will the financial effect of the decision on the public official's
economic interests be material and reasonably foreseeable?
Whether the financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's
economic interest is material and reasonably foreseeable depends upon the facts and
circumstances of each particular case. An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if
there is a substantial likelihoo dthat it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if
the effect is a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable . (In re Thorner (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 198.)
Supervisor Tony Barba
In examining the effect of the decision to adopt the Dairy Element on Supervisor
Barba's real property interests, "[t]he financial effect of a governmental decision on real
property which is indirectly involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be
material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific
circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of
the real property in which the public official has an economic interest, which make it
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the real
property in which the public official has an interest." (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1).)
Supervisor Barba's interest tn2Yz acres of land in the RRE district is not in a zone that
will be affected by adoption of the Dairy Element. Nothing in the information provided
by you otherwise indicates this real property interest will in any way be affected by the
adoption of the Dairy Element.
Supervisor Barba's indirect economic interest in his wife's 1/5 ownership interest
in 50 acres of farmland is governed by the materiality standard for real property indirectly
involved in a governmental decision. As stated above, the financial effect of the
governmental decision "is presumed not to be material." (Reg. 18705.2.) As this real
property is located in the area affected by the decision, Supervisor Barba must determine
whether the presumption of nonmateriality is rebutted. Examples of the circumstances to
be considered in this regard are.
u Th.r. holdings may also constitute a financial interest as a source of income, but no facts have
beenprovided inthatregard (g 87103(c) )
(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real property
in which the offrcial has an economic interest,
(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic interest;
(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to, substantial
effects on: traffic, view, prtyacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air emissions, or
similar traits of the neighb orhood. (Regulation I 87 0 5 .2(b)( 1 ) (A)-(C). )
If these factors are considered, and there is no material financial effect on
Supervisor Barba's interest in the real property, he can participate in the decision. As a
public official, he "must make a good faith effort to assess the effect of the decision by
using some reasonable and objective method of valuation. A public official is not
required to, by may choose to obtain a professional appraisal, such as a reai estate
appraisal, to assist in assessing the financial effect of a decision for conflict of interest
purposes." (Ho Advice Letter, No. 4-00-241.)
Supervisor Alene Taylor
In a similar vein, the 80 acres which Supervisor Taylor and her husband own are
governed by the materiality standard for real property indirectly involved in a
governmental decision and are presumed not to be material. (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1) )
However, like Supervisor Barba, her real property is in the area affected by the decision,
and she would need to examine the specific circumstances of the governmental decision
as they relate to her property to determine if the presumption is rebutted. (Regulation
1870s 2(b)(1xA)-(c) )
Her business interest in the farm operation on the land is governed by the
materiality standard for business interests that are indirectly involved in a governmental
decision. (Regulation 18705.1(b).)The financial effect of a governmental decision on the
farming operation is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; ot
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value
of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more." (Regulation
1870s t(c)(+)(a)-(c) )
While it true that the land is currently farmed in corn to be sold to the dairy
industry, as discussed below, it is highly speculative how the Dairy Element's facilitation
of construction of new dairies or expansion of existing dairies would cause a financial
effect within the scope of the materiality standard set forth above. (See 1rz re Thorner,
supra,l FPPC Ops. 198.) As the operation expects to gross only $20,000-$25,000 to
begin with, it is not reasonably foreseeable that its gross business revenues would be
increased by $20,000 because of the adoption of the Dairy Element. Further, nothing in
the facts indicates that the other two materiality factors for business would be affected by
the adoption of the Dairy Element.
With regard to her interest in her husband's co-ownership of a commerciai
excavation company, this is also governed by the materiahty standard for business
interests which are indirectly involved in a governmental decision. (Regulation
18705.1(c) ) The excavation company does $12,160 in yearly business with the county
dairy industry.It is reasonably foreseeable that the adoption of the Dairy Element will
facilitate construction of new dairies and expansion of existing dairy operations. The
same materiality standard applicable to her farming operation would be applied to her
business interest in the excavation company. (Regulation 18705. t(c)(+)(A)-(C).) Based
on the excavation business' limited reliance on the dairy industry as a source of business,
it is not reasonably foreseeable that its gross business revenues would be increased by
$20,000 because of the adoption of the Dairy Element. Further, nothing in the facts
indicates that the other two materiality factors for business would be affected by the
adoption of the Dairy Element. (See In re Thorner, ntprq,l FPPC Ops. 198.)
Supervisor Jon Rachford
Supervisor Rachford's ownership of a Subchapter S corporation which leases land
for the production of row crops is governed by the materiality standard for business
interests which are indirectly involved in a governmental decision. (Regulation
18705.1(c).) The information which you have provided to the effect that the activities of
the corporation are not in any way involved with the dairy industry supports the
conclusion that the decision on the adoption of the Dairy Element would have no
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on this business interest. (See Regulation
1870s t(c)(+)(e)-(c) )
With regard to his leasehold interest in real property farmed by the corporation,
this interest is governed by the materiality standard for leasehold interests that are
indirectly involved in a governmental decision and is presumed not to be material.
(Regulation 18705.2(b)(2).) In this regard the materiality standard and factors to be
considered are as follows:
"This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific circumstances
regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real
property in which the public official has an economic interest, which make it
reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will:
"(A) Change the legally allowable use of the leased real property, and the iessee
has a right to sublease the real property;
"(B) Change the lessee's actual use of the real property;
"(C) Substantially enhance or significantly decrease the lessee's use or enjoyment
ofthe leased real property,
"(D) Increase or decrease the amount of rent for the leased real property by 5+
percent during any L}-month period fcllc.wing the decision; or
"(E) Resultin a change in the termination date of the lease."
(Regulation t 87 0 s .2(b)(2).)
Nothing in the facts that have been provided indicates that any of these changes will be
reasonably foreseeable because of the adoption of the "Dairy Element." As such, the
decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Supervisor
Rachford's leasehold interest.
Supervisor Rachford's interests as a civil engineer, engineering consultant and
real estate broker would also be governed by the materiality standard for business
interests that are indirectly involved in a governmental decision. (Regulation 18705.1(c).)
While you have indicated that a portion of the income for these businesses comes from
agriculture-related work, you have provided additional information to the effect that to
date none of these business interests have in any way been involved with the dairy
industry. Based on the information provided, the decision on the adoption of the Dairy
Element would have no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on this business
interest. (Regulation 1 870s. t (c)(+)(a)-(C) )
Supervisor Tonv Oliveira
Supervisor Oliveira's interest in a Subchapter S corporation which operates a
dairy on land affected by adoption of the Dairy Element is governed by the materiality
standard for business interests that are indirectly involved in a governmental decision.
(Regulation 1 8705. 1(c).) One the results of adoption the Dairy Element would be that the
expansion of existing dairies would be less costly because there would be no requirement
to undertake an individual environmental review under the California Environmental
Quality Act (CEQA). In light of Supervisor Oliveira's interest in the dairy and his related
leasehold and real property ownership interests it is reasonably foreseeable that the
Subchapter S corporation in which he has an interest will at some point expand or
otherwise alter its operations in a manner in which it benefits financially from the
adoption of the Dairy Element. As such, Supervisor Oliveira must determine whether the
financial effect on his business interest in the dairy operation requires_that he disqualify
himself fromparticipating in the decision to adopt the Dairy Element.T (See Regulation
1870s t(c)(+)(e)-(c) )
With regard to his leasehold and ownership interests in land in the vicinity of that
dairy operation, these interests would be subject to the materiality standards for real
property that are indirectly involved in a governmental decision and are presumed not to
be material. Supervisor Oliveira would need to examine the specific circumstances of the
7 No information has been provided as to the actual amount of the projected cost savings under the
Dairy Element for existing dairies seeking to expand their operations.
governmental decision as they relate to his leasehold and ownership interests in the real
property to determine if the presumption is rebutted. (Regulation 18705.2(b)(1)(2) )
If these factors are considered, and there is no reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on Supervisor Oliveira's interests in the real property, he can participate
in the decision. As a public official, he "must make a good faith effort to assess the effect
of the decision by using some reasonable and objective method of valuation. A public
official is not required to, but may choose to obtain a professional appraisal, such as a
real estate appraisal, to assist in assessing the financial effect ofa decision for conflict of
interest purposes." (Ho AdviceLetter, supra, No. 4-00-241 )
Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?
In your letteq you have set forth facts suggesting application of the "public
generally" exception to the four supervisors' decision to adopt the "Dairy Element." In
further telephone conversations, you provided additional information in this regard.
Under the "public generally" exception, an official may still participate in a decision if
the financial effect of the decision on the official's economic interest is indistinguishable
from the decision's effect on the public generally. (Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).)
This "public generally" exception is codified in Regulations 18707- 18707.9. Pursuant to
these provisions, if a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction is affected by the
governmental decision in substantially the same manner as it would affect the public
official, then the official may participate in the decision.
For purposes of application of the "public generally" to a decision involving areal
property interest, a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction is defined as follows:
"(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official represents; or
"(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of the offlrcial's
asency. (Regulation 1 87 07 . 1 (bX I ) (B)(i)(iD. )
The adoption of the Dairy Element only affects areas zoned for agriculture in the
county. Of a total of 60,286 parcels owned in the county, 21,868 parcels are located in
agriculturally zoned areas. Of a total a 38,099 individual landowners in the county, 9,705
individuals own land in the agriculturally zoned areas. The adoption of the Dairy
Element will clearly affect either ten percent of all property owners or 5,000 property
owners in the county. As such, in terms of its effect on real property interests in the
county, the adoption of the Dairy Element affects a "significant segment" of the
jurisdiction.
The information that you have provided is insufficient to determine whether the
effects on Supervisor Barba's and Supervisor Taylor's interests in real property in the
agriculturally zoned area are substantially the same as the effects on the public generally.8
8 Supervisors Barba, Taylor, and Oliveira may not reach this issue if they determine that there is
no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on their real property irterests as discussed above.
(Regulation 18707(b)(4).) The effects may be different depending on the size of the
parcels. However, because Supervisor Oliveira is operating a dairy with attendant
improvements on the property and has other dairy related real property interests, all of
which will be specifically affected by the adoption of the Dairy Element in terms of
future expansion, his real property interests will be affected in a manner that is clearly
different from the effect on the public generally.
The regulations define a "significant segment" of the jurisdiction in relation to
business interests as follows:
For decisions that affect a business entity in which a public official has an
economic interest the decision also affects 2,000 or twenty-five percent of all
business entities in the jurisdiction or the district the official represents, so long as
the effect is on persons composed of more than a single industry, trade, or
profession. . (Regulation 18707.1(b)(l)(C) )
The informationthat you have provided with regard to the effect of the decision
does not establish that it also affects 2,000 or twenty-five percent of all business entities
in the county. As such, there is no basis to apply the "public generally" exception to the
decision to adopt the Dairy Element as it applies to the business interests of the
supervisors.e

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Do Kings County Supervisors Tony Barba, Alene Taylor, Jon Rachford, and Tony
Oliveira have disqualifying real property andlor a business interests that prohibit them
from participating in the decision to adopt a"Dairy Element" amendment to the county
general plan and enact related modifications to the Kings County Zoning Ordinance? 
3
2 This advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are
coffect, and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71; g
83 r 14.)
' For purposes of this letter, the term "Dairy Element" is used to describe the dairy element
amendment to the General Plan and the enactment of related modifications to the zoning ordinance.

CONCLUSION: 1. The decision to adopt the Dairy Element will not have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Supervisor Barba's real property interest outside of the
agriculturally zoned areas. As to his indirect real property interest in the agriculturally
zoned area, he can participate in the decision if he determines that it will not have
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property interest. The facts
are insufficient to determine whether his indirect interest in real property in the
agriculturally zoned area will be affected in the same manner as the public generally.
2 The decision to adopt the Dairy Element will not have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on Supervisor Taylor's business interests. As to her real property
interest in the agriculturally zoned area, she can particip ate in the decision if he
determines that it will not have reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her
real property interest. The facts are insufficient to determine whether her interest in real
property in the agriculturally zoned area will be affected in the same manner as the public
generally.
3. Supervisor Rachford may participate in the decision to adopt the Dairy
Element because it will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his
real property and business interests.
4. Supervisor Oliveira may participate in the decision if after examining the
appropriate materiality factors, he determines that adoption of the Dairy Element will
have no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his business interest in a dairy
operation and his real property interests in the agriculturally zoned areas.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 September 20, 2001 Peter D. Moock Kings County Office of County Counsel Government Center Hanford, CA 93230 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-01-150 Dear Mr. Moock: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is limited solely to provisions of the Act, and should not be taken as advice or an opinion regarding any other areas of the law potentially raised by your letter. This advice is based upon the facts as provided in your request.² Please note that this letter should not be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A) ) QUESTION Do Kings County Supervisors Tony Barba, Alene Taylor, Jon Rachford, and Tony Oliveira have disqualifying real property and/or a business interests that prohibit them from participating in the decision to adopt a "Dairy Element" amendment to the county general plan and enact related modifications to the Kings County Zoning Ordinance?³ ___________________________ ¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. ² This advice is applicable and confers immunity only to the extent that the facts provided to us are correct, and that all of the material facts have been disclosed. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; § 83114.) ³ For purposes of this letter, the term "Dairy Element" is used to describe the dairy element amendment to the General Plan and the enactment of related modifications to the zoning ordinance. File No. A-01-150 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS 1. The decision to adopt the Dairy Element will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Supervisor Barba's real property interest outside of the agriculturally zoned areas. As to his indirect real property interest in the agriculturally zoned area, he can participate in the decision if he determines that it will not have reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property interest. The facts are insufficient to determine whether his indirect interest in real property in the agriculturally zoned area will be affected in the same manner as the public generally. 2. The decision to adopt the Dairy Element will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Supervisor Taylor's business interests. As to her real property interest in the agriculturally zoned area, she can participate in the decision if he determines that it will not have reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real property interest. The facts are insufficient to determine whether her interest in real property in the agriculturally zoned area will be affected in the same manner as the public generally. 3. Supervisor Rachford may participate in the decision to adopt the Dairy Element because it will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his real property and business interests. 4.
